questions.question: What is the primary cause of this performance degradation under load when using Virtual Threads?
options: A) The Spring `@Service` proxy intercepts the synchronized method, forcing the JVM to fall back to platform threads for all invocations.
B) The blocking HTTP call inside the `synchronized` method pins the virtual thread to its carrier platform thread, preventing the carrier thread from executing other virtual threads.
C) The non-thread-safe `HashMap` triggers a JVM internal safety check, halting the virtual thread executor to avoid memory corruption.
D) Virtual threads cannot run `Supplier` functional interfaces, causing them to block permanently on the thread scheduler.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) The blocking HTTP call inside the `synchronized` method pins the virtual thread to its carrier platform thread, preventing the carrier thread from executing other virtual threads.
questions.explanation: When a virtual thread executes a blocking operation (like an HTTP request or I/O) inside a `synchronized` block or method, the virtual thread is unable to unmount from its carrier thread (a platform thread from the `ForkJoinPool`). This is known as thread pinning. Under heavy load, all carrier platform threads become pinned, leading to carrier thread starvation and severe application-wide latency spikes. To resolve this, `ReentrantLock` should be used instead of `synchronized`.

questions.question: Why does `notifier.sendEmail(order)` periodically fail to read the correct database state?
options: A) `CompletableFuture.runAsync` executes tasks on a separate thread pool concurrently, which may attempt to read the database before the main thread commits the Spring `@Transactional` transaction.
B) Spring `@Transactional` locks the entire table when `CompletableFuture` is invoked, causing a deadlock on the background thread.
C) The Spring proxy blocks any asynchronous execution of methods called inside a `@Transactional` boundary.
D) `CompletableFuture` automatically converts all downstream repository calls into read-only transactions, ignoring the parent context.
questions.correct_answer_text: A) `CompletableFuture.runAsync` executes tasks on a separate thread pool concurrently, which may attempt to read the database before the main thread commits the Spring `@Transactional` transaction.
questions.explanation: Spring's `@Transactional` manages database transactions using a thread-local association. The transaction commits only after the `processOrder` method finishes successfully. However, `CompletableFuture.runAsync` immediately spawns a background task running in the `ForkJoinPool.commonPool()` (or another executor). Because this happens on a different thread, the transaction context does not propagate, and the background thread may attempt to read the order from the database before the transaction on the main thread has committed, resulting in a dirty read or a missing record error. A transaction synchronization listener (`TransactionSynchronizationManager`) should be used to trigger the async task post-commit.

questions.question: What JVM scheduling behavior explains why this setup causes a complete and permanent freeze?
options: A) The JVM garbage collector prioritizes virtual threads waiting on semaphores, causing an OutOfMemoryError and halting the CPU scheduler.

B) The `synchronized` block pins the carrier thread during the HTTP call. When the number of pinned carrier threads reaches the carrier pool limit, no other virtual thread can run to execute `semaphore.release()`, creating a starvation deadlock.
C) `Semaphore` is explicitly incompatible with virtual threads and forces the JVM's ForkJoinPool to permanently discard task queues.
D) Virtual threads do not support the `InterruptedException` mechanism, causing the `try-catch` block to enter an infinite loop.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) The `synchronized` block pins the carrier thread during the HTTP call. When the number of pinned carrier threads reaches the carrier pool limit, no other virtual thread can run to execute `semaphore.release()`, creating a starvation deadlock.
questions.explanation: Because of the `synchronized` block enclosing the blocking network call, virtual threads pin their underlying platform carrier threads. If the number of concurrent executions exceeds the size of the carrier pool (which defaults to the number of available CPU cores), all carrier threads become pinned. Consequently, no other virtual threads—including those that have completed their database operations and are waiting to run `semaphore.release()`—can be scheduled. This creates a state of carrier thread starvation, completely halting progress across the entire system.
